g.data-narration: Welcome to learning how computers fetch data from memory, a fundamental operation in every computing device.
The CPU and memory work together. The CPU computes while memory stores instructions and data needed for operations.
Memory fetch is the first step in the fetch-decode-execute cycle, the fundamental operation driving all computer programs.
g.text: The fundamental cycle that runs in every computer

g.data-narration: Two critical registers enable memory access: MAR holds addresses, and MDR holds the actual data being transferred.
The Memory Address Register specifies which memory location to access, determining the maximum addressable memory size.
g.text: to read from or write to

g.data-narration: The Memory Data Register acts as a buffer, holding data either read from memory or waiting to be written to memory.
g.text: Data flows from Memory → MDR → CPU

g.data-narration: The Read control signal tells memory to output data. Without this signal, memory remains passive and does not respond.
First, the CPU loads the desired address into the MAR. Then it activates the Read signal, and memory decodes the address.
Memory places the data onto the bus, which gets loaded into the MDR. Then the CPU transfers this data to its internal registers.
Memory operations take time. The CPU waits for memory to respond, introducing wait states in the system clock cycle.
Let's trace fetching value 0xABCD1234 from address 2050 into register R1, completing the full memory fetch operation.
In summary: MAR holds addresses, MDR holds data, MemRead triggers reads, and this fetch operation is fundamental to computer performance.
